tasks.description: Find a specific well-known paper by topic and approximate details
tasks.input: Find the original Transformer paper — the one that introduced the 'Attention Is All You Need' architecture. Give me the full citation, where it was published, its citation count, and a one-paragraph summary of its main contribution.

scoring.5: Correctly identifies Vaswani et al. (2017), 'Attention Is All You Need', NeurIPS 2017; provides arXiv ID (1706.03762) and/or DOI
scoring.3: Identifies the correct paper but with minor metadata errors (wrong year, incomplete author list)
scoring.1: Returns a related but different paper on attention mechanisms
scoring.0: Cannot find the paper or returns irrelevant results

scoring.5: Full citation: all 8 authors, year (2017), venue (NeurIPS / NIPS), citation count from Semantic Scholar, arXiv link, publication status (peer-reviewed)
scoring.3: Partial citation with title, first author, and year but missing some metadata
scoring.1: Title and URL only

scoring.5: Accurate summary covering: self-attention mechanism replacing recurrence/convolution, multi-head attention, positional encoding, and its impact on NLP/sequence modeling
scoring.3: Correct but superficial summary mentioning attention but lacking architectural details
scoring.1: Vague or partially incorrect summary

tasks.description: Find recent papers on a well-defined topic within a single database
tasks.input: Search arXiv for the 5 most recent papers on diffusion models for image generation submitted in 2024 or later. List them with titles, authors, submission dates, and arXiv IDs.

scoring.5: Uses arXiv API with appropriate query: cat:cs.CV, ti:/abs: prefixes for 'diffusion model', sortBy=submittedDate, date constraint for 2024+
scoring.3: Searches arXiv but with suboptimal query construction (e.g., no category filter)
scoring.1: Searches Google Scholar or Semantic Scholar instead of arXiv as requested

scoring.5: Returns 5 real arXiv papers on diffusion models for image generation, all from 2024+; arXiv IDs are valid; dates are correct
scoring.3: Returns 5 papers but 1-2 are not specifically about diffusion models for images, or dates are slightly off
scoring.1: Fewer than 3 relevant papers or some entries appear fabricated
scoring.0: Results are not from arXiv or are fabricated

scoring.5: Each paper has: complete title, full author list, exact submission date, valid arXiv ID (YYMM.NNNNN format)
scoring.3: Title and arXiv ID present but author list is incomplete or dates are approximate
scoring.1: Only titles provided; missing IDs and dates

tasks.description: Find papers by a specific author on a known topic
tasks.input: Find papers by Yann LeCun on self-supervised learning published since 2020. Include citation counts and venues.

scoring.5: Correctly searches for Yann LeCun using author-specific query syntax across databases; uses au: prefix on arXiv, author search on Semantic Scholar
scoring.3: Searches for the author but only on one database
scoring.1: Searches for the topic but does not properly filter by author

scoring.5: Returns papers where LeCun is an author (not just cited); all papers are about self-supervised learning; all from 2020+; includes key works like the JEPA paper or VICReg
scoring.3: Most papers are by LeCun on self-supervised learning but 1-2 are off-topic or misattributed
scoring.1: Mix of relevant and irrelevant papers; author filtering is loose
scoring.0: Papers are not by LeCun or not about self-supervised learning

scoring.5: Each paper includes citation count (from Semantic Scholar or Google Scholar), venue name, and publication status; citation counts are plausible
scoring.3: Citation counts provided for most papers but venues missing for some
scoring.1: No citation counts; only titles and years

tasks.description: Cross-database literature search on an interdisciplinary topic
tasks.input: I'm starting a research project on using graph neural networks for drug discovery. Find the top 5 most relevant papers that cover: (1) GNN architectures for molecular property prediction, (2) applications to virtual screening or lead optimization, and (3) benchmark datasets used in this area. I need papers from top venues with citation analysis.

scoring.5: Returns papers covering all 3 requested aspects: GNN architectures (e.g., SchNet, DimeNet, GemNet), virtual screening applications, and benchmark datasets (e.g., MoleculeNet, ZINC); at least 1 paper per aspect
scoring.3: Covers 2 of 3 aspects; one aspect underrepresented
scoring.1: Only covers GNN architectures without drug discovery application or benchmarks
scoring.0: Results are generic GNN papers without drug discovery focus

scoring.5: Searches arXiv (cs.LG + q-bio.QM categories), Semantic Scholar (fieldsOfStudy: Computer Science + Biology), and Google Scholar; deduplicates across databases
scoring.3: Searches 2 databases with reasonable queries
scoring.1: Single database search only

scoring.5: Papers from top venues (NeurIPS, ICML, Nature Machine Intelligence, JCIM, Bioinformatics); includes citation counts, influential citation counts, and citation velocity for trend analysis
scoring.3: Good venues but citation analysis is limited to raw counts only
scoring.1: Mixed venue quality; no citation analysis
scoring.0: Low-quality venues or no venue information

scoring.5: Provides thematic grouping of papers, identifies the current state-of-the-art, notes key open challenges, and suggests a reading order for someone starting in this field
scoring.3: Brief per-paper summaries but no cross-paper synthesis
scoring.1: Paper list without context or guidance

tasks.description: Literature review requiring citation graph traversal
tasks.input: I found the paper 'BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding' (Devlin et al., 2019) very useful. Find 5 papers that directly build on BERT's approach — specifically papers that propose improvements to BERT's pre-training objectives or architecture. I want the papers that cite BERT and made the biggest impact. Exclude papers that merely use BERT as a baseline without modifying it.

scoring.5: Uses Semantic Scholar citation API to find papers citing BERT; filters by influential citation count; correctly identifies papers that extend (not just use) BERT's architecture or pre-training
scoring.3: Finds papers related to BERT but through keyword search rather than citation graph traversal
scoring.1: Returns general BERT-related papers without distinguishing extensions from applications

scoring.5: All 5 papers propose modifications to BERT's pre-training or architecture (e.g., RoBERTa, ALBERT, DeBERTa, SpanBERT, ELECTRA); correctly excludes papers that merely fine-tune BERT on downstream tasks
scoring.3: 3-4 papers are BERT extensions; 1-2 are BERT applications rather than modifications
scoring.1: Mix of BERT-related papers without clear distinction between extensions and applications
scoring.0: Results are generic NLP papers or not about BERT improvements

scoring.5: Papers are ranked by actual impact: citation count, influential citations, adoption in the field; explains what each paper changed about BERT and why it mattered
scoring.3: Citation counts provided but no analysis of relative impact or contribution type
scoring.1: No impact ranking or assessment
scoring.0: Impact data is missing or fabricated

scoring.5: Full bibliographic metadata for all 5 papers; open-access links; clear description of what each paper modified about BERT
scoring.3: Metadata mostly complete but missing details on specific modifications
scoring.1: Titles and authors only

tasks.description: Search requiring publication status verification and open-access resolution
tasks.input: Find 5 papers on federated learning for healthcare data published in peer-reviewed journals (not preprints or conference papers). For each paper, I need the journal name, impact factor or h5-index, DOI, and whether it's open access. If a paper is behind a paywall, find the open-access version if one exists.

scoring.5: All 5 papers are from peer-reviewed journals (not conferences or arXiv); correctly identifies and filters out preprints; uses publicationTypes filter on Semantic Scholar or equivalent
scoring.3: 4 papers are from journals; 1 is a conference paper or preprint incorrectly included
scoring.1: Mix of journals, conferences, and preprints without distinction

scoring.5: Each paper includes: journal name, impact factor or h5-index, ISSN or journal ranking; journals are relevant to healthcare AI (e.g., Nature Medicine, JAMIA, npj Digital Medicine, IEEE JBHI)
scoring.3: Journal names provided but missing impact metrics for some entries
scoring.1: Some journal names missing; no impact metrics

scoring.5: For each paper: DOI provided, open-access status checked via Semantic Scholar openAccessPdf field; for paywalled papers, checks arXiv preprint version, PubMed Central, or author's repository; provides direct PDF links where available
scoring.3: DOIs provided; open-access status noted but no effort to find alternative access for paywalled papers
scoring.1: DOIs for some papers; no open-access analysis

scoring.5: All 5 papers are specifically about federated learning applied to healthcare/medical data; covers relevant aspects (privacy, clinical outcomes, multi-site studies)
scoring.3: Most papers are relevant but 1-2 are about federated learning in general or healthcare ML without federated component
scoring.1: Generic federated learning or healthcare ML papers

tasks.description: Comparative literature search requiring multi-method analysis
tasks.input: Compare the main approaches to text-to-image generation in the academic literature: GANs vs. diffusion models vs. autoregressive models. Find 2 representative papers for each approach (6 total) and analyze which approach is currently dominant based on benchmark results (FID scores on standard datasets) and citation trends.

scoring.5: Returns exactly 2 representative papers per approach (6 total): GANs (e.g., StyleGAN, GigaGAN), diffusion (e.g., DALL-E 2, Stable Diffusion, Imagen), autoregressive (e.g., DALL-E, Parti, CogView); papers are well-chosen representatives
scoring.3: Covers all 3 approaches but paper selection is uneven or suboptimal (e.g., 3 diffusion papers, 1 GAN, 2 autoregressive)
scoring.1: Covers only 2 of 3 approaches or returns generic papers

scoring.5: Compares approaches using standard metrics (FID, CLIP score, IS) on common benchmarks (COCO, ImageNet); identifies which approach achieves state-of-the-art; notes the evolution over time
scoring.3: Mentions metrics but without systematic comparison across approaches
scoring.1: Qualitative comparison only; no benchmark numbers

scoring.5: Analyzes citation trends showing the shift from GANs to diffusion models; notes publication volume and venue trends; provides evidence for which approach is currently dominant
scoring.3: Notes that diffusion models are recent trend but without citation data to support
scoring.1: No trend analysis; static comparison only

scoring.5: All 6 papers are from top venues with full metadata; citation counts and publication years allow trend visualization
scoring.3: Good papers but incomplete metadata for trend analysis
scoring.1: Mixed quality papers; some metadata missing

tasks.description: Systematic literature search on a niche interdisciplinary topic
tasks.input: Conduct a mini systematic literature review on the use of causal inference methods in natural language processing. I need: (1) a clear search strategy documented with queries used, (2) papers categorized by causal method type (do-calculus, instrumental variables, propensity score matching, counterfactual reasoning), (3) the NLP tasks these methods are applied to, and (4) an assessment of whether results show causal methods outperform correlation-based approaches. Target the top 5 most impactful papers.

scoring.5: Documents the exact queries used for each database (arXiv: field prefixes + category codes; Semantic Scholar: query + filters; Google Scholar: operators); explains inclusion/exclusion criteria; notes how many results were screened vs. selected
scoring.3: Mentions databases searched but doesn't document exact queries or screening process
scoring.1: No documentation of search methodology

scoring.5: Papers are correctly categorized by causal method: identifies which use do-calculus/SCMs, instrumental variables, propensity scores, counterfactual reasoning, or other causal frameworks; explains each method briefly
scoring.3: Papers are grouped but categorization is imprecise or misses some method types
scoring.1: Papers listed without methodological categorization
scoring.0: No categorization or incorrect method identification

scoring.5: Clearly maps each paper to the NLP task it addresses (e.g., text classification, NLI, bias mitigation, question answering, summarization); identifies which tasks have the most causal inference research
scoring.3: NLP tasks mentioned but mapping is incomplete
scoring.1: Tasks not clearly identified

scoring.5: Synthesizes findings across papers: reports whether causal methods outperform baselines (with specific metrics), identifies conditions where causal approaches are most beneficial, notes limitations and open challenges; provides a clear conclusion
scoring.3: Summarizes individual paper findings but doesn't synthesize across papers
scoring.1: Abstracts only; no comparative analysis

tasks.description: Search requiring quality assessment of contradictory findings in the literature
tasks.input: There is an ongoing debate about whether scaling language models (more parameters, more data) is sufficient for achieving reasoning capabilities, or whether architectural innovations are needed. Find the 5 most important papers representing both sides of this debate. For each paper, assess the strength of its evidence (methodology, experimental rigor, reproducibility). Provide a balanced synthesis of the current state of the debate.

scoring.5: Returns papers from both sides: 2-3 pro-scaling papers (e.g., Kaplan et al. scaling laws, GPT-4 technical report, Chinchilla) and 2-3 pro-architecture papers (e.g., chain-of-thought, Mamba, retrieval-augmented approaches); balance is fair
scoring.3: Both sides represented but imbalanced (e.g., 4 papers from one side, 1 from the other)
scoring.1: Only one side of the debate represented
scoring.0: Papers are not about the scaling vs. architecture debate

scoring.5: For each paper: evaluates methodology (controlled experiments vs. observational), identifies baseline comparisons, notes whether claims are well-supported by data, flags potential confounds (e.g., training data contamination, compute budget differences); rates evidence strength
scoring.3: Notes methodology for some papers but assessment is surface-level
scoring.1: No evidence quality assessment; takes paper claims at face value

scoring.5: Provides nuanced synthesis: acknowledges that both sides have valid evidence, identifies conditions where scaling helps vs. where architecture matters, notes that the debate may be a false dichotomy, discusses implications for future research
scoring.3: Presents both sides but synthesis lacks nuance or makes oversimplified conclusions
scoring.1: Summaries of individual papers without connecting them

scoring.5: Papers are from top venues, highly cited, and represent key milestones in the debate; selection demonstrates deep understanding of the research landscape
scoring.3: Good papers but misses some key contributions to the debate
scoring.1: Mixed quality; includes minor papers while missing landmark ones

tasks.description: Cross-lingual and cross-domain academic search with methodology assessment
tasks.input: I need to understand the current state of research on AI-generated text detection. Find 5 key papers covering: (1) detection methods (watermarking, statistical tests, trained classifiers), (2) adversarial attacks that evade detection, and (3) evaluation benchmarks. For each paper, critically assess whether the methodology would generalize to newer models like GPT-4 and Claude. Identify the biggest gaps in the current detection literature.

scoring.5: Papers cover all 3 requested subtopics: detection methods (watermarking like Kirchenbauer et al., statistical like DetectGPT, classifiers like GPTZero papers), adversarial evasion techniques, and evaluation benchmarks; at least 1 paper per subtopic
scoring.3: Covers 2 of 3 subtopics well; one subtopic underrepresented
scoring.1: Only covers detection methods without adversarial or benchmark perspectives

scoring.5: For each paper, critically evaluates whether findings generalize to newer models: identifies which methods are model-specific (e.g., watermarking requires model access) vs. model-agnostic; discusses known failures on GPT-4-class models; distinguishes white-box from black-box approaches
scoring.3: Mentions generalizability concerns but assessment is not paper-specific
scoring.1: No generalizability analysis; assumes all methods work on any model

scoring.5: Identifies specific research gaps: detection for paraphrased AI text, cross-lingual detection, detection in mixed human-AI text, benchmark standardization, real-world deployment challenges; supported by evidence from the surveyed papers
scoring.3: Identifies 1-2 gaps but analysis is superficial
scoring.1: Generic statement about needing more research without specific gaps

scoring.5: Multi-database search with deduplication; all papers are real with complete metadata; includes mix of peer-reviewed and significant preprints; notes publication status
scoring.3: Good papers but search limited to one database; metadata mostly complete
scoring.1: Papers may be fabricated or metadata is incomplete
scoring.0: Poor search quality or fabricated results
